author: @nemo-79000
text: Putting magnets on the disc is a terrible idea. It created disruptions to the laminar flow of the boundary layer which completely disrupts the main working principal of the turbine. This is why even with a very dense medium like water the rpm rate was negligible. A gimmick at best.

author: @lastchance045
text:
Considering the need to store power from windmills and solar cells: would it be reasonable to compress air and then at a later time use the compressed air to drive air turbines?
Would the inefficency be greater than batteries? Less costly than batteries. More evironmently correct?

replies.author: @Cheebzsta
replies.text:
Substantially lower efficiency than batteries.

There are others but I'll cite three main problems:
1. Mechanical efficiency of the turbine. Most small scale turbines peak around 75%-80% mechanically efficient. In the case of a Tesla turbine they've yet to be demonstrated as functional and capable of producing in excess of 50% so it's even worse.
2. Heat loss during compression. Compressing air makes it hot and that's not ideal so you need to cool it down before storing it. This is a major source of losses.
3. Small-scale turbines have mechanical issues that make them questionable. We'll ignore that the good ones are often extremely expensive for a moment and focus on the practical problems. Small scale turbines are smaller (duh) which means they tend to run at an RPM where elaborate gearing is required which opens up issues of cost. 

If you invest in a large diameter turbine and have existing compressed air the economic problem becomes: Why not just run a natural gas plant?

Sure, it's the opposite of your point, but it's partly why: Economic incentives.

Collectively the round-trip efficiency of compressed air storage is either around or below 50% vs 95%+ for lithium ion batteries.

In short: It's certainly a tool that should be in out kit but given that there are other systems with better round trip efficiencies, like pumped hydro, hydrogen, etc, that appear more promising in the short term.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
Cool :)
speaking of compressed air- just yesterday I saw a company had made a solenoid pump that worked about 38% faster at pumping (both sides can be utilized), and 33% more efficient (due to the losses in going from rotational to linear through an arm, and where the power is in that compression stroke)  They designed it to make AC compressors use less energy, so it's only going to be industrial application.  Seemed like a notion you might find interesting so I thought I'd mention it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is interesting and I did have a look - seems the basis is a solenoid - like it though

author: @antoniopacelli
text:
Is this a clever way to say:
"Hi lad ... what you need a Stirling for.. when you have a Tesla ! ? !"
If this is the case I got your point...
But:
Making a fully functional & optimized Stirling Engine costs less than making a fully optimized Tesla turbine that doesn't break under it's own created centrifugal motion.
Carbon Nanofoam, Crabonium, Titanium are all expensive product even in large scale...
Maybe it costs less on assembling phase because it can be made as an almost fully mechanical industrial process...as per yours plastic aluminium and carbon ink idea, it get easier to mass produce the more you make it.
Ending up taking out all of the human handiwork..

But I think the Queen also value the world and works of Her working class heroes..

author: @stevetobias4890
text: So basically it comes down to the coil arrangement and the number of coils which determines the output voltage and current. (And of course the magnet size and strength)

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: yep, basically more -1mtr- turns = more v, thicker wire =more current as it has less resistance than thin stuff, magnetic tesla =combination more overlapping turns & wire thickness ,3feet/1mtr of wire is standard base calcs & about the least length to get something usable out of it too. x windings masks the actual length you might actually use so measure it before you start lol.

replies.author: @reypolice5231
replies.text:
@ Steve to

What you said has merit. Those factors matter.

However in previous videos Robert demonstrate that cheap ceramic magnets vs the expensive stronger neodymium magnets did not present a substantial gain.

What Robert pointed out was the speed/ RPM, at which it turned was what:  INCREASED voltage/ amperage/ = watts realized.

Hope that helps.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @reypolice5231 I agree with everything you said however each generator / converter has its own balance when it comes to magnets and coil arrangement. In one application one work better than it would in another.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: basically 3 factors mate - coils, magnets and speed. coils are all about number of turns and thickness of wire, magnets are all about field strength and speed - well - as. much as you can!
